description.task: Create a table with a PRIMARY KEY and demonstrate that duplicate or NULL primary key values are rejected.
instructions: Run the table creation and valid inserts.
Run the SELECT to show all students.
Uncomment the duplicate PK insert (id=2) and run it — note the error message.
Re-comment the failing insert and run the information_schema query to see constraints.
Add a comment explaining: why can a table have only ONE primary key but MULTIPLE UNIQUE constraints?
description.hint: A PRIMARY KEY = NOT NULL + UNIQUE. A table can have many UNIQUE columns but only one PRIMARY KEY (which is the main row identifier used by foreign keys).
description.expectedOutput:
3 rows: Alice(1), Bob(2), Carol(3)
Error on duplicate: 'duplicate key value violates unique constraint'

description.task: Create two related tables (departments and employees) with a FOREIGN KEY. Attempt an insert that violates referential integrity.
instructions: Run all CREATE and INSERT statements.
Run Query 1 to view employees with department names.
Run Query 2 to find departments with no employees.
Uncomment the violating INSERT (Eve, dept 99) and note the FK constraint error.
In a comment, explain: why does the FK constraint protect data integrity?
description.hint: A foreign key ensures that every dept_id in employees MUST exist in departments.dept_id. This prevents orphan records.
description.expectedOutput:
Q1: Alice/Engineering, Carol/Engineering, Bob/Marketing, Dave/HR
Q2: No department is empty (all 3 have employees)

description.task: Design and query a table that uses all four key types: PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY, UNIQUE KEY, and COMPOSITE KEY.
description.context: Understanding all key types is essential for database design. Each serves a distinct purpose in enforcing data integrity.
description.starterCode:
CREATE TABLE courses (
  course_id INT PRIMARY KEY,          -- Primary Key
  course_code VARCHAR(10) UNIQUE,     -- Unique Key (alternate key)
  title VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  credits INT
);

CREATE TABLE enrollments (
  student_id INT,
  course_id INT,
  semester VARCHAR(10),
  grade CHAR(2),
  PRIMARY KEY (student_id, course_id, semester),  -- Composite Key
  FOREIGN KEY (course_id) REFERENCES courses(course_id)
);

INSERT INTO courses VALUES
  (1, 'CS101', 'Introduction to Programming', 3),
  (2, 'DB201', 'Database Fundamentals',       3),
  (3, 'MA101', 'Calculus I',                  4);

INSERT INTO enrollments VALUES
  (1001, 1, 'Spring2026', 'A'),
  (1001, 2, 'Spring2026', 'B+'),
  (1002, 1, 'Spring2026', 'A-'),
  (1001, 1, 'Fall2025',   'B');  -- same student, same course, different semester

-- Query 1: Show all enrollments with course title (JOIN)
SELECT e.student_id, c.title, e.semester, e.grade
FROM enrollments e
JOIN courses c ON e.course_id = c.course_id
ORDER BY e.student_id, e.semester;

-- Query 2: Find courses by course_code (using Unique Key)
SELECT * FROM courses WHERE course_code = 'DB201';
instructions: Run all CREATE and INSERT statements.
Run Query 1 to see all enrollments with course names.
Run Query 2 to look up by course_code.
In comments, label which key type each constraint is.
Explain: why does the composite PK allow the same student to re-enroll in the same course in a different semester?
description.hint: The composite PK (student_id, course_id, semester) means uniqueness is checked across all THREE columns together — not individually. So (1001, 1, Fall2025) is unique even though 1001 and 1 appear in other rows.
description.expectedOutput:
Q1: 4 enrollment rows with course titles
Q2: DB201 — Database Fundamentals, 3 credits
